section.title: A Call to Persevere
section.sectionRationale: The letter turns from indictment to instruction. Jude reminds the beloved that the apostles foretold such scoffers, then gives the positive counterpart to contending: build yourselves up, pray, keep yourselves in God's love, and await Christ's mercy. Finally he directs their compassion outward, to rescue others from the fire with a mercy mixed with fear.
section.thesis: Against the divisive, Spirit-less scoffers, believers persevere by building themselves up in the faith, praying in the Spirit, and keeping themselves in God's love, and from that security they reach out to save the doubting and the endangered.
section.frameNote: Three movements: remember the apostles' warning about scoffers, verses 17 to 19; keep yourselves in God's love by faith, prayer, and hope, verses 20 to 21; and show discerning mercy to others, verses 22 to 23.

verses.text: But you, beloved, remember what was foretold by the apostles of our Lord Jesus Christ
when they said to you, “In the last times there will be {{empaiktes|scoffers}} who will follow after their own ungodly desires.”
These are the ones who cause divisions, who are worldly and {{psychikos|devoid of the Spirit}}.
But you, beloved, by {{epoikodomeo|building yourselves up}} in your most holy faith and praying in the Holy Spirit,
{{tereo|keep}} yourselves in the love of God as you await the mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ to bring you eternal life.
And indeed, have {{eleos|mercy}} on those who doubt;
save others by {{harpazo|snatching them from the fire}}; and to still others show mercy tempered with fear, hating even the clothing stained by the flesh.

empaiktes.gloss: A mocker, scoffer, one who ridicules. The apostles foretold that in the last times such scoffers would come, following their own ungodly desires. Their appearance is not a surprise but a fulfillment of what was predicted, so believers should not be shaken by it.

psychikos.gloss: Natural, worldly, governed by the unspiritual self rather than the Spirit. Despite their claims to special insight, these dividers do not have the Spirit at all. They live on a merely natural level, which is why they cause divisions in the church.

epoikodomeo.gloss: To build up upon a foundation. Believers are to keep building themselves up on the foundation of their most holy faith, the apostolic gospel. Perseverance is active and constructive, not passive; it grows on what has been once for all delivered.

tereo.gloss: To keep, guard, hold securely. In verse 1 God keeps believers; here believers are to keep themselves in the love of God. The two are not in tension: those whom God keeps are the very ones who, by His grace, keep themselves near His love.

eleos.gloss: Mercy, compassion toward the needy. Having received mercy and awaiting Christ's mercy, believers extend mercy to others, to the doubting and the endangered. Mercy received overflows into mercy shown, even amid a fight for the truth.

harpazo.gloss: To snatch, seize, or carry off quickly and forcefully. Some must be saved by snatching them from the fire, rescued with urgency from real danger. Love for the endangered is not passive well wishing but a decisive reaching in to pull them to safety.

basic.context: Jude turns from the false teachers to his beloved readers. First, remember: the apostles said scoffers would come in the last times, so do not be surprised. Then, act: build yourselves up in the faith, pray in the Spirit, keep yourselves in God's love, and wait for Christ's mercy. Finally, reach out: have mercy on doubters, snatch others from the fire, and show mercy carefully to still others, hating even the sin that clings to them.
basic.hermeneutics: The same letter that commands a fight for the faith now shows what that fight looks like from the inside: personal growth, prayer, and love, overflowing into rescue of others. The commands are prescriptive for every believer. Note the balance of God's keeping and our keeping in verses 1 and 21: God guards us, and by His grace we keep ourselves near His love.
basic.originalLanguages: The apostles foretold empaiktes, scoffers. The dividers are psychikos, worldly, without the Spirit. Believers are to epoikodomeo, build themselves up, and to tereo, keep, themselves in God's love, the same verb used of God keeping them. They show eleos, mercy, and even harpazo, snatch, others from the fire.
basic.typology: Snatching people from the fire echoes the rescue of Lot from Sodom and the picture in Zechariah of a brand plucked from the burning. The believer's work of urgent rescue reflects the greater rescue God has worked in Christ, pulling sinners out of judgment.
basic.christ: Believers wait for the mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ that brings eternal life. He is the coming Savior whose mercy is the church's hope. The mercy we have received from Him, and the mercy we await from Him, become the pattern and the power for the mercy we show to others.
basic.god: We keep ourselves in the love of God, a love that is the safe home of the believer. God is the one who loves, who keeps, and who will bring His people to eternal life through Christ's mercy. Perseverance is not white knuckled effort but abiding in a love that holds us fast.
basic.crossReferences: Second Peter 3 and Acts 20 record the apostles' warnings about scoffers and wolves. Romans 8 speaks of praying in the Spirit. And Zechariah 3 pictures a brand snatched from the fire, the urgent rescue Jude urges.
basic.oneStory: God has always kept a people for Himself through every season of danger. Jude shows how: rooted in the once for all faith, praying in the Spirit, abiding in the Father's love, and awaiting the Son's mercy, God's people persevere, and reach out to snatch others from the fire, until Christ brings them home to eternal life.

context.literary: The hinge from denunciation to exhortation, marked by the affectionate but you, beloved, repeated in verses 17 and 20, deliberately contrasting the readers with these men of the previous section. Verses 20 to 21 form a compact structure with keep yourselves at the center, surrounded by building, praying, and awaiting. Verses 22 to 23 give three objects of mercy.
context.historical: The apostolic warning about scoffers, verse 18, closely matches 2 Peter 3:3, reflecting a shared apostolic message the churches had received. The reference to clothing stained by the flesh, verse 23, may draw on Zechariah 3, where filthy garments picture defilement, urging both compassion and caution toward those entangled in sin.
context.canonical: This movement gathers the New Testament's teaching on perseverance, growth in the faith, prayer in the Spirit, and abiding in God's love, and joins it to the church's mission to rescue the perishing, all under the hope of Christ's return.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To equip the beloved to persevere amid false teaching by remembering the apostles' warning and by actively keeping themselves in God's love, and to direct their compassion outward in discerning rescue of others.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses 17 to 19 describe the foretold scoffers; verses 20 to 23 are strongly prescriptive, a series of commands for how believers are to live and love. These commands hold for the church in every age facing the same dangers.

debates.question: How do we reconcile God keeping us, verse 1, with the command to keep ourselves, verse 21?

views.label: Two sides of one grace
views.case: God's keeping is primary and secure, verses 1 and 24, and it works itself out through our active keeping, verse 21. Those whom God preserves are enabled and commanded to persevere; the command is the means by which the promise is fulfilled, as in Philippians 2:12-13.

views.label: A genuine human responsibility
views.case: Others stress that the command is real and must not be softened: believers truly must keep themselves in God's love, using the means Jude names, or drift into danger.

debates.bearing: The best reading holds both without contradiction: assurance rests on God's keeping, while that assurance fuels rather than cancels our diligent keeping. Grace does not make us passive; it makes us persevere.

debates.question: What are the three groups in verses 22 to 23, and how are they treated?

views.label: Three distinct groups needing tailored mercy
views.case: The doubting are to be shown gentle mercy, the endangered are to be urgently snatched from the fire, and the dangerously entangled are to be helped with mercy mixed with fear, hating the sin that stains them. Discernment shapes how love acts.

views.label: A general call to varied compassion
views.case: Some read the distinctions more loosely as one call to wise, differentiated mercy toward those affected by false teaching, without pressing sharp boundaries between the groups.

debates.bearing: Either way the passage teaches that love must be both bold and careful: eager to rescue, yet watchful not to be defiled, matching its approach to each person's need and danger.

study.originalLanguages: The section pivots on tender address and active verbs. The apostles foretold empaiktes, scoffers, so their coming should steady rather than shake the church. The dividers are psychikos, merely natural and Spirit-less, for all their pretended insight. Believers, by contrast, epoikodomeo, build themselves up on the most holy faith, and pray in the Spirit, and then tereo, keep, themselves in the love of God, the very verb God used of keeping them, showing that His preserving grace and our diligence run together. From that security flows eleos, mercy: gentle to doubters, and urgent enough to harpazo, snatch, the endangered out of the fire, yet careful enough to hate even the garment stained by the flesh.

typology.fulfillment: Echoes the rescue of Lot and the brand plucked from the burning, Zechariah 3:2, and pictures the church's urgent share in Christ's rescue of sinners from judgment.

typology.type: Keeping oneself in the love of God
typology.fulfillment: Fulfills the covenant call to abide in God's love, realized in Christ's own words, Abide in My love, John 15:9-10.

study.christ: The movement is oriented toward Christ's return: believers await the mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ that brings eternal life. He is the Savior whose past mercy has redeemed them and whose future mercy is their sure hope. Because they have received His mercy and look for more, they can afford to be merciful to others, even the doubting and the endangered. And the love of God in which they keep themselves is the love supremely shown and secured in Him.
study.god: God is the Father whose love is the believer's dwelling place, keep yourselves in the love of God. He is not a taskmaster driving anxious effort but the one whose love holds His people and calls them to remain within it through faith, prayer, and hope. His purpose is life: through the mercy of Christ He brings His own to eternal life, and He shapes them into a people who extend that same merciful heart to a perishing world.

crossReferences.note: The apostolic prediction of scoffers in the last days, following their own evil desires, which Jude recalls.
Paul warns the Ephesian elders that fierce wolves will come, urging watchfulness, akin to Jude's remember.
Abide in My love, the pattern behind keeping yourselves in the love of God.
A brand snatched from the fire and filthy garments removed, imagery echoed in snatching others and hating the stained clothing.

study.oneStory: Through every age of danger God has preserved a people, and Jude shows the shape of that perseverance: built on the faith once delivered, praying in the Spirit, abiding in the Father's love, awaiting the Son's mercy. From that safe place the church turns outward to snatch others from the fire. It is the same rescuing love that runs through the whole Bible, flowing from the God who keeps His own all the way to the eternal life Christ's mercy secures.

sermon.title: Keep Yourselves
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Jude, this message expounds Jude 1:20-21, the heart of the call to persevere: building up in the faith, praying in the Spirit, and keeping oneself in the love of God while awaiting Christ's mercy. A clear, orthodox handling of this movement.

quiz.q: Why does Jude tell believers to remember what the apostles foretold?

options.text: So the coming of scoffers steadies rather than shakes them, since it was predicted.
options.note: Jude 1:17-18 grounds the church against surprise: the scoffers fulfill apostolic warning.

options.text: So they can identify exactly which year the scoffers would arrive.
options.note: The point is expectancy and steadiness, not calculating dates.

options.text: Because the apostles were unsure whether trouble would come.
options.note: The apostles clearly predicted scoffers; there was no uncertainty.

options.text: So they would feel free to ignore the danger.
options.note: Remembering leads to vigilance and perseverance, not complacency.

quiz.q: How does Jude describe the dividers in verse 19?

options.text: Worldly and devoid of the Spirit, despite any claims to special insight.
options.note: Jude 1:19 calls them psychikos, natural, not having the Spirit at all.

options.text: Spiritually mature but socially awkward.
options.note: The text denies they have the Spirit; maturity is not the issue.

options.text: Sincere believers who simply love debate.
options.note: They are described as Spirit-less dividers, not sincere believers.

options.text: Gifted teachers with minor flaws.
options.note: Their flaw is fundamental: they cause divisions and lack the Spirit.

quiz.q: What is at the center of Jude's call to persevere in verses 20 to 21?

options.text: Keep yourselves in the love of God, by building up in faith, praying in the Spirit, and awaiting Christ's mercy.
options.note: Jude 1:20-21 frames keeping oneself in God's love with faith, prayer, and hope.

options.text: Withdraw entirely from anyone who struggles.
options.note: The next verses call for merciful outreach, not withdrawal.

options.text: Rely on feelings to know you are saved.
options.note: Jude points to concrete means of grace, not mere feelings.

options.text: Work hard enough to earn God's love.
options.note: We keep ourselves in a love already given, not earn it by effort.

quiz.q: How do God keeping us and our keeping ourselves fit together?

options.text: They are two sides of one grace: God preserves us, and that assurance fuels our diligence.
options.note: Verses 1, 21, and 24 hold God's keeping and our keeping together, as in Philippians 2:12-13.

options.text: They contradict each other, so one must be false.
options.note: Scripture holds both without contradiction; grace enables our perseverance.

options.text: God's keeping makes our effort pointless.
options.note: His keeping does not cancel the command; it empowers it.

options.text: Our keeping earns God's keeping in return.
options.note: Our keeping flows from His grace; it does not purchase it.

quiz.q: How does Jude say believers should treat others in verses 22 to 23?

options.text: With discerning mercy: gentle to doubters, urgent to snatch the endangered, careful with those entangled in sin.
options.note: Jude 1:22-23 calls for tailored, bold yet watchful compassion.

options.text: With harsh condemnation for everyone affected.
options.note: The command is mercy, not blanket condemnation.

options.text: With total indifference to those in danger.
options.note: The opposite: snatch them from the fire with urgency.

options.text: With unlimited mercy that ignores all danger of sin.
options.note: Mercy is real but discerning, hating even the clothing stained by the flesh.

questions: Jude says to build yourselves up in the faith and pray in the Spirit. What concrete habits help you grow, and which one could you strengthen this week?
Believers are told to keep themselves in the love of God, a love already given. What does it look like for you to abide in that love rather than strive for it?
God keeps us, and we keep ourselves. How does resting in God's keeping actually energize your effort rather than replace it?
Jude calls for mercy toward doubters and urgency toward those in danger. Who around you needs gentle patience, and who needs a bolder rescue?
We are to show mercy while hating even the sin that clings to people. How can you love those caught in sin without being pulled into it yourself?

translationNotes.note: This apostolic saying closely parallels 2 Peter 3:3, reflecting a warning the churches had already received from the apostles.
Devoid of the Spirit renders psychikos, meaning natural or unspiritual; the same word Paul uses in 1 Corinthians 2:14 for the person without the Spirit.

The manuscripts vary in how they divide these groups, but the sense is a threefold, discerning mercy: gentle to the doubting, urgent to snatch the endangered, and careful with those entangled in sin. Hating the clothing stained by the flesh echoes Zechariah 3.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Jude 1:17-23. No dashes appear in this movement's text.
